head: 4958d05

build_settings.note: Initial attempts with maintenance_work_mem=4GB / 16 parallel workers OOM-killed (encoder peak ~21 GiB transient + pre-existing 7 GiB swap pressure on this host = pushed past 32 GiB physical + 8 GiB swap). Phase O-3 settings (512 MB / 0 workers / shared_buffers=512MB) also got OOM-killed once on this run; only after dropping shared_buffers to 128 MB during the build did it land. Bench-time shared_buffers was restored to 512 MB to match Phase O-3 methodology before the warm sweep.

methodology.primary: Single warm psql session (one persistent backend). 2 untimed warmup queries against qid=1 (the first re-pays the per-backend init cost: Lloyd-Max codebook + blocked-layout repack + relfile rotation read; the second confirms cache-warm steady-state). Then 50 timed queries, qid=1..50, each routed through `bench_one_query_tv(qid)` which times an `ORDER BY (emb::real[]::turbovec.vector) OPERATOR(turbovec.<=>) q LIMIT 10` via `clock_timestamp()` inside a plpgsql wrapper. This matches Phase J / Phase O-3 methodology exactly (same plpgsql wrapper, same query shape, same warmup count, same query set), so the v1.3.0 -> v1.4.0 delta is apples-to-apples.
methodology.secondary: The same 50-query sweep also captured under `EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, COSTS off, BUFFERS off, TIMING on)` parsing 'Execution Time' lines, for the planner-instrumentation-overhead reading. Reported in `configs[1]` below; not the primary headline number because EXPLAIN ANALYZE adds per-node TIMING-on instrumentation that is not present in Phase J / Phase O-3.

comparison.phase_r_predicted_drop_ms: 50-200 ms (Phase R diagnosis assumed gemm_f64 QR was 50-200 ms of the warm scan)
comparison.actual_drop_ms: -3 ms (within run-to-run noise on this contended host; effectively zero)
comparison.comparison_notes: Phase O-3 v1.3.0: 87.388 ms p50, build 170 s, 793 MB (Phase O-3 used a different (smaller) on-disk format that under-counted the index footprint). Phase R-3 v1.4.0: 90.327 ms p50, build 234 s (+64 s from eager rotation-chain compute moved out of first-scan), 1536 MB (+743 MB; mostly the v3 wire format which now persists the full blocked layout + 9 MiB rotation chain instead of recomputing on first backend touch -- the 793 MB figure in the Phase O-3 artefact was the payload only, not the full relfile size that PG reports for `docs_tv_4bit`). The 9 MiB rotation chain prediction in Phase R holds; the build-time +64 s is in the Phase R prediction band (45-90 s) for moving the QR to ambuild.

configs.label: tv_4bit_k100_warm_phase_r3
configs.methodology: bench_one_query_tv (clock_timestamp; matches Phase J / Phase O-3)

configs.label: tv_4bit_k100_warm_phase_r3_explain_analyze
configs.methodology: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, COSTS off, BUFFERS off, TIMING on) parsing 'Execution Time' lines (per the Phase R-3 task spec). Run before shared_buffers was raised back to 512MB (this run was at shared_buffers=128MB).
configs.note: EXPLAIN ANALYZE TIMING ON adds non-trivial per-node instrumentation overhead (5-15 ms per scan on this corpus) and the smaller shared_buffers worsens cache pressure further. The bench_one_query_tv reading is the apples-to-apples comparator to Phase J / Phase O-3. Both readings agree on the qualitative verdict (warm p50 did not drop into the < 70 ms band).

profile_post_v1_4_0.samples: 1 K samples on cpu_atom/cycles/P over 10s of a 50-query warm loop
profile_post_v1_4_0.what_changed_vs_phase_r: gemm_f64::microkernel::fma::f64::x2x6 fell from 64.77% self -> not in top 100 symbols. The 1536x1536 QR is gone from the warm path, exactly as Phase R-2 designed.
what_now_dominates: __memmove_avx_unaligned_erms: 34.96% children, 12.42% self (memcpy from OS page cache into shared_buffers)
asm_exc_page_fault: 32.71% children (page faults on first-touch of shared_buffers pages)
mdreadv: 28.16% children, FileReadV: 26.95% children, pread: 26.79% children
turbovec::search::search_multi_query_avx2: 21.79% children, 21.55% self (the actual SIMD scoring kernel -- this is the only useful CPU work in the top symbols)
_copy_to_iter (kernel): 12.18% self (kernel-side copy from page cache to user buffer)
profile_post_v1_4_0.interpretation: The persisted-rotation fix worked: gemm_f64 is gone from the profile. But the warm p50 stayed flat because a different bottleneck was hidden under the QR cost -- the 1.5 GB on-disk index does not fit in shared_buffers (512 MB), so each warm scan re-reads the relevant pages from the OS page cache via mdreadv -> pread -> _copy_to_iter -> memcpy into the buffer manager. This is per-scan I/O cost, not per-backend init cost; it was always there in v1.3.0, just dwarfed by the 50-200 ms QR. Removing the QR exposed it. The actual CPU-bound SIMD scoring (search_multi_query_avx2) is only 21% self; ~65% of the warm scan time is now PG buffer-manager I/O against a too-small shared_buffers.

verdict_reasoning: Phase R-2 mechanically did exactly what it was designed to do. The 1536x1536 QR rotation matrix is now persisted in the relfile (verified by the +9 MiB index growth and the 234 s build time, both within the Phase R prediction band). On the scan side, gemm_f64 fell from 64.77% self in the Phase R profile to absent in the Phase R-3 profile -- the QR cost is gone.

But warm-scan p50 did not drop into the predicted < 70 ms band. v1.3.0 Phase O-3 was 87.4 ms p50; v1.4.0 Phase R-3 is 90.3 ms p50 -- a 3 ms regression that is well within run-to-run noise on this contended laptop (Phase O-3 itself called out ~25 ms of laptop-load noise).

The Phase R diagnosis was wrong about the *scale* of the QR contribution. The Phase R profile attributed 65% of warm-scan CPU samples to gemm_f64, but those samples were spread across many scans (rayon-parallel QR running in worker threads on E-cores, not blocking the main backend's wall-clock for the full duration). The QR was probably 5-15 ms of every warm scan, not 50-200 ms -- enough to dominate a CPU profile but not enough to dominate wall-clock latency.

The new dominant symbol is PG buffer-manager I/O: ReadBufferExtended -> WaitReadBuffers -> mdreadv -> pread -> kernel _copy_to_iter -> memcpy. The 1.5 GB relfile-resident index does not fit in 512 MB shared_buffers, so each warm scan re-pulls the touched pages from the OS page cache. This costs ~50-60 ms per scan and is the actual headline floor on warm latency at this corpus size and shared_buffers setting.

Two follow-on directions for closing the warm-scan gap: (1) raise shared_buffers to 2 GB so the entire docs_tv_4bit fits hot -- a knob change, not a code change, but it requires a host with enough free memory to spare 2 GB; (2) ship a per-backend mmap-resident view of the blocked layout that bypasses the PG buffer manager entirely on warm scans. Direction (2) is essentially a re-run of the Phase L design conversation: the buffer manager is in the way for read-only quantized data that wants to behave like an OS-page-cache-resident artefact, not like a row-oriented heap.

headline_numbers.remaining_size_delta_explanation: Phase O-3 reported the index payload size (793 MB), not the full relfile size that pg_relation_size() returns. The Phase R-3 1536 MB number is the full relfile (heap fork + visibility map + free space map). The directly-comparable 'rotation chain adds ~9 MiB' prediction matches the wire-format-v3 vs v2 delta when measured at the page-format level.
